---
title: "Difference-in-Differences"
subtitle: "From the classic 2x2 to modern heterogeneity-robust estimators"
---

## Overview

Difference-in-differences (DiD) is one of the most widely used quasi-experimental designs in applied economics. The core idea is simple: compare the change in outcomes for a treated group to the change for a control group. If the two groups would have followed the same trend absent treatment --- the parallel trends assumption --- the difference in their changes identifies the causal effect.

This module starts with the classic 2x2 design, extends it to regression and staggered treatment adoption, and covers the modern literature on heterogeneity-robust estimators.

## Pages in This Module

- [Classic 2x2 DiD](classic-2x2.qmd) --- The simplest case: two groups, two periods.
- [Regression DiD](regression-did.qmd) --- Implementing DiD in a regression framework.
- [Parallel Trends](parallel-trends.qmd) --- Testing, assessing, and defending the key assumption.
- [Staggered Adoption](staggered.qmd) --- When treatment rolls out at different times.
- [Modern Estimators](modern-estimators.qmd) --- Callaway-Sant'Anna, Sun-Abraham, and others.
- [Diagnostics](diagnostics.qmd) --- Event studies, pre-trend tests, and sensitivity analysis.
